metadata.notes: Master plant file with structured image roles. First batch had 13 image-ready species from existing hardwired entries; this batch adds sourced and curated image sets for 3 more species (American beech, ramps, basswood). Copyback content merged directly into plants.json from batch10 for 74 matching species on 2026-04-21. Plant data normalization pass on 2026-04-29 added explicit usable_parts, plant_lanes, plant_lane_note, plant_card_note, and plant_data_flags fields for all records. Plant lane correction pass on 2026-04-29 tightened Leaves/Greens and Flowers to actual useful parts, limited the tree/sap lane to trees/shrubs, merged Sassafras root bark into Sassafras, and improved Wild mustard greens. Plant quality/correctness pass on 2026-04-29 filled weak copyback records for dandelion, hawthorn, plantain, pawpaw, pin cherry, spruce, and jewelweed; tightened safety wording for sassafras; improved wild mustard greens source data; and merged duplicate pine and wood-sorrel part records. Follow-up in the same pass cleared remaining old copyback placeholders for ramps, oak, wild carrot, wild ginger, wild strawberries, and yellow pond lily where safe, while flagging caution-heavy entries for review. Also filled toothwort and yarrow season/habitat/use basics and flagged yarrow safety wording for review. v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split active data sources by size; record bodies and source order preserved.
metadata.split_note: v4.3.0-r2026-05-11-data-chunk-size-cleanup1 split this active data source by file size to keep working chunks under roughly 75–100 KB. Record bodies and source order were preserved.

records.notes: Moist-ground annual with watery stems, alternate toothed leaves, orange spotted pouch-shaped flowers, and spring-loaded seed capsules.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food plant in this guide; any edible-flower note needs verification before this belongs in the Plants food list.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional topical use for itching/skin irritation is the main use context.
reviewReasons: Food-side use is questionable; may belong outside the edible Plants section.
records.plant_lane_note: Kept temporarily in Flowers but flagged because the food-side reason is weak.
records.plant_card_note: Good image record and common wet-ground plant, but food-side use is questionable. Candidate to move out of Plants unless a solid edible-use source is added.
records.edibility_detail: Flagged: food-side usefulness is weak/questionable. This may belong in Medicinal/Other Uses rather than the edible Plants list.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with white spring flowers and blueberry-like fruits ripening to purple.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food fruit in this app.
records.culinary_uses: Sweet berries are eaten fresh or used in pies, jams, syrups, and dried fruit mixes.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully colored and sweet.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully colored and sweet.

records.notes: Low shrub or small conifer with sharp needle-like leaves and blue berry-like cones.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Berry-like cones are used sparingly as seasoning, tea, or flavoring rather than bulk food.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional aromatic and urinary herb used in teas and preparations.
records.edibility_detail: Used in small amounts as flavoring or tea. Not a bulk fruit.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Used in small amounts as flavoring or tea. Not a bulk fruit.

records.notes: Bog shrub with leathery alternate leaves and rusty underside, plus clusters of white flowers.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass as a warning-aware northern medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves are used as tea in traditional northern practice rather than as a vegetable food.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tea plant with aromatic and medicinal history.
records.edibility_detail: Tea use is traditional, but this is a use-with-knowledge plant rather than a carefree daily beverage.
look_alikes: Bog rosemary and other bog shrubs
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / tea, flower / tea. Tea use is traditional, but this is a use-with-knowledge plant rather than a carefree daily beverage.

records.notes: Variable weedy plant with often whitish-mealy new growth and triangular to goosefoot-shaped leaves.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food weed here.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves are cooked like spinach and seeds are also edible with processing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible young leaves and shoots, especially cooked.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Nuts / Seeds.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, seed / nut / cone. Edible young leaves and shoots, especially cooked.

records.notes: Low shrub of barrens, dry woods, and openings with sweet blue berries.
records.short_reason: Split from the generic Blueberries bucket into a real Upper Michigan species entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry.
records.culinary_uses: Excellent fresh berry and one of the best wild berries for baking, jam, syrup, and freezing.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. One of the best practical wild blueberry species.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. One of the best practical wild blueberry species.

records.notes: Low northern berry bucket for tart cranberry-like fruits of bog and barrens habitats.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly fruit use in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Tart red berries used for sauce, jam, syrup, and mixed preserves rather than casual handful eating.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, usually best cooked or sweetened.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, usually best cooked or sweetened.

records.notes: This consolidated entry is about recognizing the tree and understanding sap season. Sugar maple is the best-known source, but the bucket is kept under maple in the new app.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food tree in this guide, though sap has traditional strengthening and spring-tonic associations.
records.culinary_uses: Sap is tapped in late winter and early spring for drinking fresh, concentrating into syrup, or boiling into sugar.
records.edibility_detail: Sap is the practical use. Tree identification matters before tapping, and yield varies by species and weather.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: sap. Sap is the practical use. Tree identification matters before tapping, and yield varies by species and weather.

records.notes: Compatibility bucket for the winter-purslane/miner’s-lettuce lane kept from the older app structure.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a spring salad green entry.
records.culinary_uses: Tender juicy greens used fresh in salad or lightly cooked.
records.scientific_name: Claytonia perfoliata / Montia perfoliata style bucket
records.edibility_detail: Edible tender greens; the practical point is mild spring salad use.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, stem / shoot / stalk. Edible tender greens; the practical point is mild spring salad use.

records.notes: Tree with blackberry-like aggregate fruits borne singly or in loose groups.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Primarily a fruit tree entry.
records.culinary_uses: Soft sweet berries are eaten fresh or used in pies, jam, syrup, drying, and wine.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, but very perishable and messy.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, but very perishable and messy.

records.notes: Shrub or small tree with opposite leaves, flat-topped white flower clusters, and dangling dark fruits that sweeten after frost or full ripening.
records.short_reason: Added in plant expansion pass for Upper Michigan edible and medicinal species.
records.culinary_uses: Blue-black ripe fruits can be eaten fresh when soft or used in preserves, leather, and cooked fruit preparations.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional shrub with some medicinal history, though primarily a food fruit here.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully dark and soft.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit. Best when fully dark and soft.

records.notes: Opposite pointed leaves with stinging hairs on rich disturbed ground, edges, and moist places. Harvest with gloves and positive identification.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional tonic plant with urinary, nutritive, and general restorative use context; seeds also have small-quantity tonic use.
records.culinary_uses: Young tops are a serious spring green once cooked, blanched, dried, or otherwise processed; seeds can also be dried and used in small amounts in herb blends or foods.
records.edibility_detail: Must be cooked, blanched, dried, or otherwise processed before eating. Raw plants sting skin and can irritate the mouth.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens, Nuts / Seeds, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, seed / nut / cone. Must be cooked, blanched, dried, or otherwise processed before eating. Raw plants sting skin and can irritate the mouth.

records.notes: Broad oak entry focused on acorns. Oaks are trees/shrubs with lobed or toothed leaves and acorns in caps; acorn tannin levels vary by species.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Not treated as a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Acorns can be shelled, leached to remove tannins/bitterness, dried, and used as meal or flour.
records.plant_lane_note: Acorns are the useful part; this is a processing-heavy food, not a quick nibble.
records.plant_card_note: Acorns are usable after shelling and leaching tannins; best treated as flour/meal, not a raw snack.
records.edibility_detail: Acorns must be processed/leached before eating. Do not eat bitter raw acorns as a casual snack.

records.notes: Shrubby true blueberry with oval leaves and soft-blue fruit.
records.short_reason: Split from the generic Blueberries bucket into a real Upper Michigan species entry.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food berry species here.
records.culinary_uses: Sweet ripe berries are good fresh, baked, or preserved.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit.

records.notes: Small understory tree with large tropical-looking leaves, dark purple spring flowers, and soft greenish-yellow fruits in fall. Native in Michigan but more southern than the U.P. core list.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Not treated as a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Fully ripe fruit is eaten fresh or used in soft desserts and pulp preparations; it is highly perishable.
reviewReasons: U.P. relevance questionable; likely more southern Michigan than core Upper Peninsula foraging.
records.plant_lane_note: Fruit is the only food-side part tracked here; U.P. relevance is questionable enough to keep flagged.
records.plant_card_note: Largest native North American fruit, but this is more of a Michigan/southern-range entry than a solid U.P. core species. Keep flagged for U.P. relevance.
records.edibility_detail: Fruit pulp is the edible part. Do not treat seeds, skin, leaves, or bark as food. U.P. wild occurrence should be reviewed.

records.notes: Small pioneer tree with reddish-brown bark marked by horizontal lenticels, narrow toothed leaves, white spring flowers, and small bright red sour cherries in clusters.
records.short_reason: Species-level pin cherry record retained without changing slug wiring.
records.medicinal_uses: Not treated as a medicinal plant in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Small sour fruits are mainly useful for jelly, syrup, preserves, or cooked fruit preparations.
records.plant_lane_note: Fruit is the useful part; flowers are listed for identification timing, not as a food lane.
records.plant_card_note: Small sour red cherries ripen in mid-to-late summer and are better for jelly or preserves than fresh eating. Avoid pits and non-fruit parts.
records.edibility_detail: Ripe fruit flesh is edible and sour. Do not treat pits, leaves, bark, or wilted foliage as food.

records.notes: Consolidated pine entry replacing separate pine needles and pine resin/gum records. The tree must be recognized first; safe use depends on knowing which pine you have.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Needle tea and resin/gum have traditional respiratory, aromatic, and topical-use associations; this guide treats those as traditional-use context.
records.culinary_uses: Needles/tips are used for tea and flavoring; some species provide edible nuts; resin/gum has traditional chew/pitch use but is not a bulk food.
records.edibility_detail: Needle tea is the most approachable use. Pine nuts depend heavily on species, and resin/gum use is more traditional than routine food.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Nuts / Seeds, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Needles/tips are the practical tea/flavoring use; some pines provide nuts, and resin/gum is more traditional than routine food.

records.notes: Low perennial lawn and path weed with a basal rosette of broad oval leaves, strong parallel veins, and leafless flower/seed spikes.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Traditional soothing/topical leaf use exists; in this guide it is mainly tracked as an edible green and tea plant.
records.culinary_uses: Young tender leaves can be eaten raw or cooked; older leaves get fibrous and are better cooked or used as tea.
records.plant_lane_note: Young leaves are the main edible part; dried leaves can be used for tea, and seed use is secondary.
records.plant_card_note: Young leaves are the practical edible part; older leaves become fibrous but can be cooked or used for tea. Look for the basal rosette and strong parallel veins.
records.edibility_detail: Best as young leaves from clean, unsprayed ground. Older leaves are tougher and stringier.

records.notes: Low sprawling succulent with reddish stems and fleshy leaves, common in disturbed sunny ground.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Some traditional use, but mainly a food green.
records.culinary_uses: Succulent stems and leaves are used raw or cooked in salads, sautés, and pickles.
records.edibility_detail: Edible and crisp, especially when young and fresh.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green, stem / shoot / stalk. Edible and crisp, especially when young and fresh.

records.notes: Spring ephemeral wild onion of rich deciduous woods, often in colonies. Broad leaves emerge early, then die back as the plant flowers and goes dormant.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a food allium in this guide.
records.culinary_uses: Leaves and bulbs are edible and strongly onion/garlic flavored, but harvest should be conservative; leaf-only harvest is less damaging than digging bulbs.
records.plant_lane_note: Leaves and bulbs are useful, but the leaves are the more sustainable practical harvest.
records.plant_card_note: Spring wild onion of rich woods; leaves and bulbs are edible, but leaf-only harvest is the better conservation habit.
records.edibility_detail: Leaves and bulbs are edible. Sustainability matters: avoid stripping patches, and harvest lightly if at all.

records.notes: Cane-forming bramble with aggregate fruits that separate from the core when picked.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly a food berry, though leaf tea traditions exist in some contexts.
records.culinary_uses: Classic fresh berry also used in jam, syrup, desserts, drying, and fruit leather.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit. This broad entry stands in for the general raspberry bucket.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs, Tea / Infusions.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry, leaf / tea. Edible ripe fruit. This broad entry stands in for the general raspberry bucket.

records.notes: Clustered translucent red berries on a currant shrub with lobed leaves.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Mostly a fruit shrub entry.
records.culinary_uses: Bright tart berries are excellent for jelly, syrup, preserves, and baking.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe berries, though many people prefer them sweetened or processed.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits, Trees / Shrubs.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe berries, though many people prefer them sweetened or processed.

records.notes: Small sour dock with narrow arrow-like leaves, common in poor soils and open disturbed places.
records.short_reason: Original app species restored into the standalone modular build.
records.medicinal_uses: Minor medicinal history; mostly an edible tart green here.
records.culinary_uses: Tangy leaves used raw or cooked in small amounts for salads, soups, and sauces.
records.edibility_detail: Edible in small amounts; tartness is the point.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Leaves / Greens.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: leaf / green. Edible in small amounts; tartness is the point.

records.notes: Wild river-edge grape with climbing habit and clustered dark fruits.
records.short_reason: Consolidated to one plant entry with details covering the usable parts.
records.medicinal_uses: Mainly a fruit vine entry.
records.culinary_uses: Small tart grapes used in jelly, juice, wine, and cooked preserves.
records.edibility_detail: Edible ripe fruit, usually best processed.
records.plant_lane_note: Explicit plant-lane placement: Berries / Fruits.
records.plant_card_note: Useful parts: fruit / berry. Edible ripe fruit, usually best processed.
